1: The speech-introduction formula wayyaʿan + name + speech-type marker is the standard poetic frame for all dialogue rounds (cf. Job 4:1; Job 8:1). Temanite (tēmānî) marks Eliphaz as a sage of the south Arabian wisdom tradition.
2: Rûaḥ qāḏîm (east wind, lit. 'wind of the east') denotes the sirocco, the hot dry desert wind that scorches crops. Eliphaz accuses Job of filling himself with dāʿat rûaḥ — 'empty wind-knowledge': fine-sounding but hot and destructive. Ḥākām (wise man) sets up the irony: is this how a sage speaks?
3: Millîm lōʾ yôʿîl: 'words that accomplish nothing' — the term yôʿîl (Hiphil of yāʿal) means 'to profit/benefit'; wisdom speech must produce tangible good. The wisdom tradition prizes utility: speech that edifies vs. speech that merely fills time.
4: Yirʾat ʾēl (fear of God): the foundational wisdom category (cf. Job 1:1, 8; Proverbs 1:7). Eliphaz charges Job with undermining this fear — tāpar (to loosen, weaken) — and with impeding śîḥāh (meditation/prayer/reflection) before God. The accusation is serious: Job's speech is not merely imprudent but anti-pious.
5: ʿĂwōnĕkā (your iniquity/guilt) from ʿāwōn, the standard OT word for moral deviation and its consequences. Lĕšôn ʿărûmîm (tongue of the cunning/crafty): Eliphaz links Job's speech to the serpent's wisdom (ʿārûm, Genesis 3:1), a damning insinuation.
6: Pîkā (your mouth) and śĕpātêkā (your lips) are the instruments of self-incrimination. The legal language — mouth/lips as witnesses (ʿānāh bĕkā, 'testify against you') — frames Job's speech as courtroom evidence. Eliphaz claims not to be the accuser; Job's own words are.
7: Hārîšôn āḏām tiwwālēḏ: 'Were you the first man born?' Rîšôn (first) may allude to a primal wisdom figure — the primordial man of Ezekiel 28:12-14 or the figure behind Proverbs 8:22-31 — someone present at creation. Eliphaz mocks Job's implicit claim to unique insight.
8: Sôḏ ʾĕlôah: the 'council/secret of God' — the heavenly assembly where divine deliberations occur (cf. Job 1:6; Amos 3:7; Jeremiah 23:18). Tišmaʿ (did you hear/listen?) implies participation: Eliphaz denies that Job has been admitted to the divine council that might explain his suffering.
9: The rhetorical questions mah yādaʿtā (what do you know?) / mah tābîn (what do you understand?) challenge Job's epistemic standing. Bîn (discern/understand) is the deep wisdom term alongside ḥāḵam; Eliphaz claims parity or superiority in both.
10: Śêbāh (gray-haired) and yāšîš (very aged): the elders who anchor wisdom in generational tradition. Both terms emphasize extreme age. Kabbîr from kbr (great in years) intensifies: these men make even Job's father seem young. Tradition as epistemology.
11: Tanḥûmôt ʾēl: 'consolations of God' — the term tanḥûmāh (comfort, consolation) from nḥm (comfort, console). Eliphaz claims Job has refused divine comfort. Dābār laʾaṭ (gentle/soft word) emphasizes that God has spoken mildly to Job; Job should have received it gratefully.
12: Mah-yiqqāḥăkā libbĕkā: 'What carries your heart away?' — lēb (heart) as the seat of will and judgment. Mah-yirzmûn ʿênêkā: 'Why do your eyes flash/glare?' — rāzam (to wink, flash provocatively, HALOT) appears only here; the gesture implies arrogance or aggressive dismissal.
13: Rûḥăkā ʾel-ʾēl: 'you turn your spirit against God' — the charge of hostility toward God (cf. Job 7:11). Hôṣēʾtā (you bring out) — Job's words are portrayed as weapons brought out of the mouth rather than wisdom offered from the heart.
14: Mah-ʾĕnôš kî-yizkeh: 'What is a mortal that he could be pure?' — ʾĕnôš (mortal, frail humanity) and zāḵāh (to be clean, morally pure). Yĕlûd ʾiššāh (born of woman) is a creaturely limitation phrase (cf. Job 25:4; Sir Job 10:18; Matthew 11:11) — birth from a woman signals creaturely constitution. Eliphaz uses this anthropological premise to ground universal moral limitation.
15: Qĕdōšāyw (his holy ones): the heavenly beings/angels (cf. Job 4:18; Job 5:1). The claim that God trusts neither angels nor the heavens themselves extends moral impurity to the entire created order. Yēʾāmēn (put trust/confidence in): Niphal of ʾāman — even the celestial realm is not sufficiently reliable in God's sight.
16: Nĕtʿāb (abominable) and neʾĕlāḥ (corrupt, vile — hapax): the escalating scale of human depravity from creaturely limitation (v14) to active wickedness. Šōteh kĕmayim ʿawlāh: 'drinks wickedness like water' — the image of wicked behavior as an involuntary drink, absorbed naturally and constantly.
17: Ḥăwîtĕkā (I will show you) from ḥwh: the wisdom declaration-formula introducing Eliphaz's extended portrait of the wicked man's fate. Ḥāzîtî (I have seen): appeals to personal experience as well as tradition (v18-19).
18: Lōʾ kāḥādû: 'without concealment' — the ancestral wisdom was openly transmitted, not hidden. The wisdom tradition claims public, verifiable transmission in contrast to esoteric knowledge. Mēʾăbôtāyw (from their fathers): multigenerational authority.
19: Lāhem lĕḇaddām: 'to them alone' — the tradition belonging to those whose land was exclusively theirs before foreign contamination. The phrase implies ethnic-intellectual purity: the wisdom Eliphaz recites is pre-syncretistic, untouched by foreign influence. A claim to authentic transmission.
20: Mityaqqēl (twists in anguish): Hithpoel of ḥwl (to writhe in pain — used for labor pain and anguish). Rāšāʿ (wicked man): the standard OT legal-moral term for the guilty/condemned. Mispār šānîm (number of years): an appointed span, fixed for the tyrant (ʿārîṣ, lit. 'violent/ruthless one').
21: Qôl pĕḥādîm bĕʾoznāyw: 'a sound of terrors in his ears' — paḥad (terror, dread, sometimes of theophany) is what the wicked man carries. Šôḏēḏ (destroyer): the divine agent or personified disaster that strikes even in šālôm (peace/prosperity).
22: Lōʾ yāʾmîn: 'he has no hope' — from ʾāman (to trust/believe): the wicked man cannot trust that he will escape darkness. Ṣāpûn (reserved/marked) for the sword: the certainty of destruction already appointed against him.
23: Nōdēḏ (wanders): from nwḏ (to wander aimlessly, flee) — the exile-motif. The wicked man wanders like a fugitive. Yôm-ḥōšeḵ (day of darkness): the day of judgment/death already nākôn (established, ready) at his hand.
24: Ṣar ûmĕṣûqāh: 'distress and anguish' — the paired terms for extreme pressure. Tikpĕhû (overpower him): from kph (to subdue, overwhelm) — like a king advancing to war. The wicked man's anticipated judgment already assails him psychologically.
25: Nāṭāh yāḏô ʾel-ʾēl: 'he stretched out his hand against God' — the gesture of aggressive defiance. Yitgabbār (he defies/acts powerfully against): Hithpael of gbr, reflexive intensification — he makes himself strong against the Almighty.
26: Yārûṣ ʾēlāyw bĕṣawwāʾr: 'charging neck-forward' — a warrior's attack posture. Gabbê māginnîm (thick boss of shields): the convex central boss, the strongest defensive point. The wicked man attacks God's strongest point — an image of suicidal futility.
27: Kissāh pānāyw bĕḥelĕḇô: 'his face covered with fat' — wealth-fattened, obese prosperity. Pimaḥ-ʿal-kāsel (padding on his loins): the physical description of the prosperous wicked man. Eliphaz identifies his portrait with the very condition Job once had.
28: Yiškôn ʿārîm nĕḥārābôt: 'he inhabits ruined cities' — an ironic reversal. The man who built settlements will himself end up in desolate ruins. Bāttîm lōʾ-yēšĕḇû (houses no one inhabits): the ḥērem-formula of total desolation applied to his dwelling.
29: Lōʾ-yeʿĕšar (not remain wealthy): the wealth is temporary. Miqlāh (holdings/property): hapax; the sense of productive land. The three negatives in rapid succession (lōʾ × 3) drive home the total reversal.
30: Lōʾ-yāsûr miḥōšeḵ: inescapable darkness. Rûaḥ pîw (breath of his [God's] mouth): divine speech as destructive wind — the same instrument that creates (Genesis 2:7) also destroys the wicked man's flourishing.
31: ʾal-yaʾmēn baššāw: 'Let him not trust in emptiness' — šāwʾ (vanity, emptiness, falsehood) is the term Ecclesiastes uses for the futility of earthly gain. The warning is that the wicked man's self-trust is misplaced in something that will prove worthless.
32: Lōʾ bĕyômô: 'before its time' — premature completion/fulfillment of doom. Kappātô lōʾ-tarʿănennāh: 'his branch will not stay green' — the kappāh (frond/branch) as the symbol of flourishing life cut off before maturity.
33: Yĕḥabbēl kaggefen: 'he sheds like the vine' — ḥbl (Piel: to act corruptly, or here: to shake off) — the vine drops its unripe grapes. Kazzayit (like the olive) drops its blossoms prematurely — the destruction of potential, not just actuality.
34: ʿĂḏat ḥānēp: 'assembly of the godless' — ḥānēp (godless, profane, polluted) is a strong term for religious apostasy. ʾēš (fire) as divine judgment on ʾohŏlê šōḥaḏ (tents of bribery/corruption): the institutional wickedness brought to ruin.
35: Hārāh ʿāmāl: 'they conceive trouble' — the pregnancy-metaphor for wickedness: ʿāmāl (toil, trouble, oppression) conceived and birthed as ʾāwen (iniquity, wickedness). Biṭnām tākîn mirmāh: 'their inmost being produces deceit' — the inner life as the womb of evil.
